primary_diagnosis.name: Severe primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (first-episode oral herpes simplex virus infection) with odynophagia and early clinical dehydration from inability to maintain oral intake

differential.diagnosis: Severe primary herpetic gingivostomatitis (primary oral HSV infection)
differential.evidence_basis: A systemic prodrome (malaise, mild fever) followed by prodromal lip tingling/burning, then grouped vesicles on the lips that erupt into painful ulcers with haemorrhagic crusting, PLUS diffuse gingival erythema/swelling and widespread intra-oral ulceration, in a first-ever episode of a young adult — the defining constellation of a severe primary oral HSV infection (gingival involvement and systemic upset mark it as primary rather than a mild recurrent cold sore).

differential.diagnosis: Early dehydration / inability to maintain oral intake
differential.evidence_basis: ~18 hours of zero oral fluid intake from odynophagia with reduced urine output, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia and weakness — the functional emergency that drives disposition regardless of the underlying viral cause, and which telehealth cannot correct.

differential.diagnosis: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / erythema multiforme major (severe mucocutaneous reaction)
differential.evidence_basis: Severe painful mucosal erosions with haemorrhagic lip crusting can be the mouth involvement of SJS/EM major; must be actively screened for (new culprit drug, skin target lesions, eye/genital mucosal involvement) because it changes management entirely and cannot be examined remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Deep-space throat infection / airway compromise (retropharyngeal or peritonsillar abscess, epiglottitis)
differential.evidence_basis: Odynophagia with drooling can signal a deep-space or supraglottic infection threatening the airway; screened for with breathing difficulty, stridor, muffled voice, trismus and neck swelling — an emergency telehealth cannot exclude by examination.

Also causes painful oral ulcers with fever, but typically spares the gingiva and is accompanied by vesicles on the hands, feet and buttocks; the prominent gingivitis, haemorrhagic lip crusting and absence of acral lesions argue against it.

Causes painful oral ulcers but not grouped vesicles on the external lips, high fever, systemic prodrome or diffuse gingival inflammation, and is usually recurrent rather than a dramatic first episode.

An enteroviral cause of painful posterior-oropharyngeal vesicles/ulcers with fever, but characteristically spares the anterior mouth, gingiva and lips, unlike this diffuse anterior + gingival + lip picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is a severe primary oral HSV infection; the important-not-to-miss considerations are the dehydration functional emergency, the SJS/EM-major mucocutaneous mimic, and a deep-space/airway process — these drive the red-flag screen and the disposition rather than diagnostic doubt.

differential.diagnosis: Severe primary herpetic gingivostomatitis with early dehydration
differential.evidence_basis: First episode, systemic prodrome, grouped lip vesicles/ulcers with haemorrhagic crust, diffuse gingivostomatitis; no new drug and no extra-oral skin/eye/genital involvement (against SJS), and normal breathing/voice with no neck swelling (against a deep-space/airway process) — leaving a severe primary HSV infection whose 18-hour fluid cessation and reduced urine confirm early dehydration.

Argued against by the absence of any new culprit drug, target skin lesions or ocular/genital mucosal involvement, but retained as a mandatory safety-net because it cannot be fully examined for over telehealth and would radically change management if it emerged.

Screened negative now (normal breathing, no stridor, normal voice, no trismus, no neck swelling) but retained as a mandatory airway safety-net given odynophagia and drooling — telehealth cannot examine the throat/airway.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes the SJS and airway mimics on current information and confirms a severe primary oral HSV infection with dehydration. The dehydration and the un-examinable mimics mean this is NOT routine cold-sore self-care — it needs urgent same-day in-person assessment, escalating to ED if fluids cannot be maintained.

differential.diagnosis: Severe primary herpetic gingivostomatitis with early dehydration from odynophagia
differential.evidence_basis: Classic severe primary oral HSV constellation with a functional inability to maintain hydration; the correct telehealth end-point is recognition + urgent same-day in-person assessment (for a supervised fluid trial, topical analgesia and a prescription antiviral) with ED escalation for failure to rehydrate, plus explicit safety-netting for the SJS and airway mimics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is to recognise a severe primary oral HSV infection AND the dehydration/management emergency it creates for telehealth: arrange urgent same-day in-person care rather than home self-care, safety-net the SJS and airway/deep-space mimics, and escalate to ED for IV rehydration if the patient cannot tolerate any fluids or deteriorates. Both under-triage to routine cold-sore self-care and reflexive 000 for a stable patient are scored errors.

red_flags.description: Complete inability to swallow fluids (odynophagia) with ~18 hours of zero oral intake and drooling of saliva to avoid painful swallowing.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A patient who cannot maintain any oral hydration will progressively dehydrate; telehealth cannot deliver topical anaesthesia, a supervised fluid trial or IV fluids, so this mandates urgent in-person care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration, acute kidney injury and avoidable hospital admission for IV rehydration.

red_flags.description: Signs of early dehydration — markedly reduced urine output, dry mucous membranes, weakness and postural lightheadedness (with clinician-measured tachycardia HR 105 and Temp 38.1 on the sealed side).
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective volume depletion in a young adult who cannot drink — the driver of the urgent disposition and the trigger for ED escalation if it worsens.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised worsening dehydration progressing to hypovolaemia and acute kidney injury.

red_flags.description: Severe mucocutaneous drug reaction mimic — Stevens-Johnson syndrome / erythema multiforme major (screen: new culprit drug, target skin lesions, eye/genital mucosal involvement).
red_flags.clinical_significance: SJS/EM major is a dermatological emergency requiring the culprit drug to be stopped and urgent in-person/specialist care; a severe mucosal eruption must be screened for it, which telehealth cannot fully examine.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed SJS/TEN progresses to extensive mucocutaneous necrosis with high morbidity and mortality.

red_flags.description: Deep-space throat infection / airway compromise (retropharyngeal or peritonsillar abscess, epiglottitis) — screen: breathing difficulty, stridor, muffled voice, trismus, neck swelling.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Odynophagia with drooling can signal a supraglottic/deep-space infection threatening the airway — a true emergency telehealth cannot exclude by examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed evolving airway obstruction can be rapidly fatal.

pathophysiology_summary: Primary herpetic gingivostomatitis is the first, symptomatic infection with herpes simplex virus (usually HSV-1) in a person with no prior immunity. After a 3-5 day incubation the virus replicates in oral epithelium, producing a systemic prodrome (malaise, fever, tender regional lymphadenopathy) and localised prodromal tingling/burning, followed by crops of intraepithelial vesicles on the lips and throughout the mouth. The vesicles rupture to leave shallow, exquisitely painful fibrin-covered ulcers, with haemorrhagic crusting on the lips and a diffuse, boggy gingival erythema and swelling that distinguishes the primary infection from a mild recurrent labial 'cold sore'. Because the whole mouth is involved, pain on swallowing (odynophagia) can be severe enough to abolish oral intake — the mechanism that in this case has produced ~18 hours of zero fluids, reduced urine, dry mucous membranes and tachycardia (early hypovolaemia), with a risk of progression to significant dehydration and acute kidney injury. The illness is self-limiting over 10-14 days and an oral antiviral (aciclovir/valaciclovir) shortens the course of a severe primary infection if started early, but the immediate threat is the inability to maintain hydration. The presentation also mimics, and must be screened against, a severe mucocutaneous drug reaction (Stevens-Johnson syndrome / erythema multiforme major) and — because of odynophagia with drooling — a deep-space or supraglottic infection threatening the airway; neither can be excluded by a remote consultation, which is why the telehealth-correct disposition is urgent in-person assessment rather than home management.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating this as trivial recurrent labial herpes and advising routine home self-care, missing that a severe PRIMARY gingivostomatitis with 18 hours of no fluids and reduced urine is a functional emergency needing urgent in-person hydration care.

Focusing on naming the rash and prescribing an antiviral while overlooking that the patient cannot swallow and is dehydrating — the antiviral does nothing for the acute fluid deficit, which is what drives the disposition.
Failing to screen for a new culprit drug, target skin lesions and eye/genital mucosal involvement, and so missing a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction masquerading as mouth ulcers.
Not asking about breathing difficulty, stridor, muffled voice, trismus or neck swelling in a patient with odynophagia and drooling, and so missing an evolving deep-space or supraglottic infection.

Attempting to autonomously prescribe the antiviral and topical anaesthetic or to talk the patient through a fluid trial over chat, when topical pharyngeal anaesthesia carries an aspiration risk and needs in-person supervision, and prescribing here requires a prescriber — the correct move is to arrange urgent in-person care.

Over-escalating a haemodynamically stable, alert patient with no airway compromise straight to a 000 ambulance (T5), when urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) with ED escalation for failure to rehydrate (T4) is the proportionate disposition.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact vital signs without a home device (source in-person values BP 108/68, HR 105, Temp 38.1, RR 18, SpO2 98%, Wt 68 kg)
Capillary refill (~3 seconds) and true hydration status / degree of volume depletion
Detailed examination of the oral cavity, palate and oropharynx and palpation of regional (submandibular) lymphadenopathy
Direct assessment/exclusion of a deep-space or supraglottic (airway) process
Delivery of topical anaesthesia, a supervised fluid trial, or intravenous rehydration
Serum urea/electrolytes/creatinine to quantify dehydration or acute kidney injury
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a severe primary oral HSV infection with early dehydration from an inability to swallow; arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (after-hours GP / urgent care) able to provide topical analgesia, a supervised fluid trial, an oral antiviral and, if needed, IV rehydration; screen the SJS/EM-major and airway/deep-space mimics; and escalate to the emergency department (T4) for IV fluids if the patient cannot tolerate any oral fluids or shows worsening dehydration, or to 000 (T5) for any airway compromise. Do not attempt to manage at home with self-care alone, and do not autonomously prescribe over chat.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the underlying diagnosis (the primary-HSV constellation is classic). The dominant risks are UNDER-triage (dismissing a severe primary infection with dehydration as routine cold-sore self-care) and UNDER-screening the SJS and airway mimics that telehealth cannot examine; a secondary risk is OVER-escalation to 000 for a haemodynamically stable patient.
